advaita.rendering: Among the Sāmans, I am the Bṛhat-sāman — the preeminent one (pradhāna) among all Sāma melodies. Among metres (chandas), I am the Gāyatrī — specifically the Gāyatrī verse distinguished by its metre-form (chandoviśiṣṭa). Among months I am Mārgaśīrṣa; among seasons, the flower-bearer (kusumākara), spring.
advaita.doctrinal_note: Śaṅkara lists these vibhūtis tersely as exemplars of excellence (prādhānya) in their respective categories — each is Brahman insofar as it possesses the highest degree of that category's defining quality. The enumeration is pedagogical, pointing the discerning student toward the one non-dual substratum underlying apparent multiplicity.
advaita.bhashya_anchor: Śaṅkara bhāṣya present; bṛhatsāma = pradhāna; Gāyatrī described as chandoviśiṣṭānām ṛcām gāyatrī ṛk.

viśiṣṭādvaita.rendering: Among the Sāmans I am the Bṛhat-sāman; among metres, the Gāyatrī; among seasons, Kusumākara — spring. Each of these represents a form in which Bhagavān's pervasive sovereignty (sarvaiśvarya) shines most luminously, sustaining the devotee's contemplative gaze.
viśiṣṭādvaita.doctrinal_note: Rāmānuja's commentary is brief here, confirming the identification without extended dialectic. Within Viśiṣṭādvaita, the vibhūtis are not mere examples of excellence but genuine modal expressions of Bhagavān, the inner controller (antaryāmin) animating each — and the devotee (bhakta) who recognizes spring's beauty as Kṛṣṇa is performing worship through perception itself.
viśiṣṭādvaita.bhashya_anchor: Rāmānuja bhāṣya present but terse: lists bṛhatsāma, gāyatrī, kusumākara without extension.

dvaita.rendering: Among Sāmans, I — Hari, the supreme independent reality — am the Bṛhat-sāman, in which the lordship of the highest sovereign is hymned. Among metres, the Gāyatrī; among seasons, spring. The jīva who hears these names uttered by Bhagavān knows that dependence (pāratantrya) on Hari is the unchanging truth of all manifold excellence.
dvaita.doctrinal_note: Madhva did not comment on this verse (bhāṣya absent in panel). Rendering is constructed from Dvaita's core commitments: Hari is the sole utterly independent reality (svatantra); every vibhūti manifests His excellence while remaining wholly distinct and dependent. The Bṛhat-sāman's praise of the supreme lord maps naturally to Hari's sovereignty.
dvaita.bhashya_anchor: Madhva bhāṣya explicitly absent per payload. Rendering inferred from Dvaita doctrine; flagged accordingly.

śuddhādvaita.rendering: Among Sāmans I am the Bṛhat-sāman — the melody sung to the verse 'tvām iddhi havāmahe,' through which Indra is praised as the lord of all lords, thus displaying pre-eminence (śraiṣṭhya). Among mantras, the Gāyatrī is My vibhūti because it reveals My own form (matsvarūpapratipādakatva) and is to be meditated upon by My devotees. Among months, Mārgaśīrṣa — the season of fulfilment for the maidens' vow, fit for enjoyment. Among seasons, Kusumākara — spring — because śruti ordains it as the season for sacred fire and because it perpetually pervades Vṛndāvana.
śuddhādvaita.doctrinal_note: Vallabha's bhāṣya links each vibhūti to a specific śruti reference or liturgical context, grounding the rapturous recognition in Vedic authority. The mention of Vṛndāvana for spring is uniquely Puṣṭi-mārga: Kṛṣṇa's līlā-landscape is itself spring's highest instantiation, and all joy in nature is prasāda of that ever-present divine play.
śuddhādvaita.bhashya_anchor: Vallabha bhāṣya well-attested; references tvām iddhi havāmahe ṛc, kumārikā vow, Vṛndāvana, vāsante agnīn ādhīta śruti.

bhakti.rendering: Among the Sāmans, I am the Bṛhat-sāman — sung to the verse 'tvām iddhi havāmahe,' whereby Indra's universal lordship is praised, establishing its pre-eminence (śraiṣṭhya). Among metres I am the Gāyatrī — supreme because it generates the second birth of twice-borns (dvijatva) and because of the soma-retrieval (somāharaṇa) myth. Among seasons, Kusumākara, spring.
bhakti.doctrinal_note: Śrīdhara's bhāṣya follows the liturgical-philological method: the Bṛhat-sāman's pre-eminence is established by its ritual function in praising sovereignty; the Gāyatrī's pre-eminence by its role in dvija initiation and the Vedic soma narrative. Devotional inflection appears in the recognition that these highest forms point toward the devotee's Bhagavān as their inner ground.
bhakti.bhashya_anchor: Śrīdhara bhāṣya present; dvijatva, somāharaṇa for Gāyatrī; tvām iddhi ṛc for Bṛhat-sāman.

advaita-bhakti.rendering: Having said 'of the Vedas I am the Sāmaveda,' here is a further specification: among the Sāman melodies — chants mounted on Ṛg verses — I am the Bṛhat-sāman, sung at the atirātra sacrifice as the pṛṣṭha-stotra praising Indra as the lord of all lords. Among metres I am the Gāyatrī — supreme by reason of generating the second birth, pervading the three savanas, and the celebrated Śatapatha account in which the Gāyatrī alone retrieved the soma that Jagatī and Triṣṭubh failed to bring. Among months, the auspicious Mārgaśīrṣa, abundant in new rice. Among seasons, Kusumākara — spring, the season for upanayana, for sacred fires, for Soma-sacrifice, for joy even among the inert — as the śāstras repeatedly declare.
advaita-bhakti.doctrinal_note: Madhusūdana synthesizes Advaita's non-dual ground with Kṛṣṇa-bhakti's devotional warmth: the Gāyatrī is expounded through extensive Śatapatha and Chāndogya śruti-citation (chandogya: 'gāyatrī vā idaṃ sarvam'), while spring is validated by five or six śāstra injunctions — the density of Vedic reference itself becomes a form of worship.
advaita-bhakti.bhashya_anchor: Madhusūdana bhāṣya richly attested; Śatapatha soma-retrieval myth, Chāndogya 'gāyatrī vā idaṃ sarvam,' atirātra pṛṣṭha-stotra, multiple vāsante śāstra injunctions.

so_what_questions: Why does Kṛṣṇa identify Himself with particular excellence rather than with each thing equally — what does selectivity in the vibhūti-list teach about how Brahman (or Bhagavān) is to be recognized in the world?
The Bṛhat-sāman praises Indra as sarvaiśvara, yet Kṛṣṇa claims it — what does this say about the relationship between the Vedic devas and the Gītā's one Supreme?

The Gāyatrī is cited by Śaṅkara, Śrīdhara, Vallabha, and Madhusūdana all for different reasons (metre-excellence, dvija-initiation, soma-retrieval, matsvarūpa-revelation) — does the same sacred utterance genuinely mean all these things, or is polysemy here a problem to be resolved?

Mārgaśīrṣa is identified as the pre-eminent month — a calendrical claim rooted in agricultural and liturgical abundance — how does temporal excellence relate to the timeless (nitya) nature of Brahman?
Spring (vasanta) appears in Vallabha as the season that 'perpetually pervades Vṛndāvana' — is this a metaphorical intensification or a literal doctrinal claim about sacred geography?

Each school gives a different justification for the Gāyatrī's supremacy; what does this divergence reveal about whether vibhūti-recognition is primarily a matter of learning, liturgy, or direct experience?
If a practitioner cannot recognize Kṛṣṇa in the most beautiful thing of each category, does the teaching of vibhūti-yoga have a practical method, or is it purely a contemplative pointer?

everyday_applications.advaita: When you encounter something of arresting excellence — a piece of music, a mathematical proof, a moment of natural beauty — train the discriminating faculty (viveka) to pause and ask: what is the substratum that makes this the best of its kind? That inquiry, honestly pursued, is itself a form of the jñāna-mārga.
everyday_applications.viśiṣṭādvaita: In your work or family life, identify the one person, tool, or practice that is clearly the best available to you right now. Recognize and honour it — not as mere utility, but as the mode in which Bhagavān is most visibly present in that domain today.
everyday_applications.dvaita: When you are tempted to take credit for excellence — your best work, your finest hour — recall that Hari is the sole independent ground of all excellence and yours is a dependent reflection. Offer the praise explicitly: 'this is yours, not mine.'
everyday_applications.śuddhādvaita: Find one sensory delight this season — a flower, a scent, a texture of light — and dwell in it as Kṛṣṇa's prasāda rather than as a possession to be extended or replicated. Let the enjoyment itself be the worship.
everyday_applications.bhakti: Each morning recite the Gāyatrī with the conscious intention that the very metre carrying the mantra is Bhagavān's form — let the form of the utterance, not only its semantic content, be the object of devotion.
everyday_applications.advaita-bhakti: When the śāstras (Gītā, Upaniṣads, a teacher's words) converge on a single point from multiple angles — as Madhusūdana cites five śruti passages for spring — treat that convergence itself as evidence of a deeper unity and let it deepen rather than complicate your practice.

self_assessment: Six genuinely divergent renderings with distinct doctrinal anchors; Madhva absence flagged; Vallabha and Madhusūdana bhāṣyas richest — panel is honest, bounded, no placeholders.
